speaker: interviewer
text: --closed up properly?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 16, 1995. I'm interviewing Wilhelmina De Groot. And my name is Stella [? Eliesri. ?] This is tape number two. Mimi, that's your nickname.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my nickname.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a young woman with small children, what were your hopes for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that, you know, that my children will grow up healthy, be able to earn a living, and lead a-- lead a normal life. That they would do as the Lord demands them to do, that they will be normal, good children. That's all I wished for.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did things begin to change for you in Belgium? When did you get a sense that something was-- that the war was approaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I knew before I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in England, the first German Jews who escaped from Germany came over to London, where we lived. We used to go swimming with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1933, when-- '32, when they began in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and what did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me the things they did to girls who were walking in the street. They had buckets of acid, and they'd put the girls' hands in the buckets of acid in the street, and throw stones at all the Jewish shops. And they told me a lot of things. And this man himself had chain marks around his neck. And he came over with his little girl because his wife-- his wife was killed before they came here-- they came to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe those stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, because I, I, I knew it was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share those stories with other people in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brothers were there. My brothers used to go swimming with us. Yes. They used to tell us quite a lot of things of what they did at that time, in '32, when they first began.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us then-- so you knew that things were changing already in 1932, but here it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not knowing it was going to go as far as-- when I got married, or that I was going to Europe. I never thought of those things. I thought that would only happen in Germany and it was finished, you know? You didn't know it was going to develop into such a terrible things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did things changed for you in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we-- I was married in '40, when we heard that the war broke out, then we tried to-- then we packed our bags and we went to France, like the [? ambulance. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it that you left Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Germans were going to come in, and we knew what was going to happen--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think they might do to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kill us. We didn't know there were going to be gassed and sent to camps, but I knew they were going to, to kill the Jews because of this person coming to England when I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other people among whom you lived worry that such a thing might happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They believed--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think almost everybody was worried. A lot people left. People who had money, they left Belgium at once. But people who had children and no money, they had to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, how did you prepare yourself to leave to France? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just packed a few clothes and left with my parents-in-law and with my brother-in-law, with his baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you prepare food for the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have time for that. We just left. And we hired-- in France, on, on the border, we had a, a sort of a bicycle made for five or six people-- it was a pleasure bicycle. And we had it, and we all-- we got in. And I had my baby on my lap. Louis had Ronnie on his lap, and my parents-in-law sat at the back with my brother-in-law and his baby. And we pedaled right up, right into France. And at that night, we slept in a barn where the people we rented this thing from came to find us and took it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the beginning of the flight.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- was it someone who you knew who lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: To hire the bike from?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. No. The barn. Did you know the people who lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just farmers. Just farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They let you sleep there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they knew the Germans were coming. And they knew what was happening. It was a lot, a lot of refugees coming in at that time. It was full of refugees coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would it have made a difference, do you think, if they knew that you were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wouldn't have made any difference because they didn't start with the Jewish people until 1942. So that wouldn't have made any difference to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were you in the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other people? Strange people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know, maybe another five or 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They walked. They all walked there with their packs on their backs and all sorts of things they could save. My husband took his typewriter with him. And the straps cut right through his shoulders until they were bleeding. He wouldn't let go of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he feel he needed a typewriter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was-- it was something he thought was valuable, that we could use later on for a living, or something. I don't know. I don't know what goes through anybody's brain when they take something along.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you? Was there something special you brought with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for your children, was there something special that you brought?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. They were small.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in that barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barn? Only that one night. And then we left early the next morning to go further, as far away from the Germans as you could get.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you-- how long did you travel then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled-- before we settled-- before the Germans came in, we must have traveled about a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came to a village and the snipers came down, the German snipers. And they were shooting at all the immigrants. And my mother-- we went to look for bread. There was no food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband and I and my oldest son, my baby, we went to look for food. And my gran-- my in-laws, with my youngest son, they went in another cellar. And in between, the snipers came down, and we went into a cellar.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it was-- we had a priest in the cellar who was praying, and all the dust was coming down. The bricks were coming down. So we thought my other son had been killed in the other cellar. Well, when we got up, we flew-- we ran down the street to the other cellar. We ran over dead bodies as if it was bag-- bags of sand. We didn't even notice they were dead. And we've got to the other cellar, it was the only cellar standing, and the people in still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we left as quick as you could leave, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: And went on to the next town. I think in the next town, we stayed for a while, until the Germans took over that town, and then we had to go back. With a cart-- horse and a cart, we went back to Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you afraid that might be dangerous, to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't think of it like that because they only started with the Jewish people in 1942. Nobody stopped us. Nobody said anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what to do when you came back to Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband earned a living with selling things from here to there. [CLEARS THROAT] With another friend of his. And we, we existed-- or just existed, trying to live, and tried to make the best of things. But we didn't really know what was going to happen in 1942, that we were all going to get caught and gassed. That we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a young housewife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they kept that very, very secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did you-- was there any hint of that in the newspapers, on the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there weren't any newspapers. There weren't any papers at all. No-- we weren't allowed to have radios, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother-in-law helpful to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law?

speaker: interviewer
text: She lived with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she-- no. She lived-- they lived alone. They lived in another apartment. She lived with my brother-in-law and his, and his baby. We lived in our little house my mother gave us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things change?

speaker: interviewee
text: When things changed, when we heard they're beginning with the Jewish people. And we had a friend called Joss. He was a salesman also. And he came over and helped us. And he took me-- Louis was sent to a camp, and I was there with the children. While in the camp, I had to go to the German officers to show that my husband was working in a camp somewhere, and that they pay you a few, a few francs a week for that. But they never paid. We went, but they just wanted to keep an eye on us that we were still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of camp was that, that Louis went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in [NON-ENGLISH]. That-- no, that was in Leuven, L-E-U-V-E-N. And they took-- they had to go to a camp. But he escaped with friends of his, and went to a hotel in Leuven, where he stayed for a while until he was caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel-- how did you find that your husband had been taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he didn't come home that night. Anybody who didn't come home one night, they were caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to the authorities to look for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I knew he was caught. I packed my bag. I packed things for-- I had a girl living with me who used to be my maid before the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: But she became a friend. Her name was Dinneke. Dinneke Lambretchts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about how she fit into your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She used to be the maid before. She used to look after the kids and help me in the house. And after-- when the Germans said there's no-- Jewish people aren't allowed to have any help in the house, so she left. But after a while, she came back without anybody knowing, and she wanted to stay with me. So she lived with us. But when we went to Brussels, Joss took us to Brussels-- myself and my children, because Louis was gone already-- and we didn't dare to tell her where we were going in case the Germans got it out of her, where we were living. So we didn't tell her even. We just disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it that you went to Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were starting with all the Jewish people in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Brussels--

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends at the end of the road were taken away the night before. They just came in and took you away, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think Brussels would be more safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They didn't know our name. We changed our name to Grooten. And we have a different name on the-- took the name on a different-- the flat on a different name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us why you changed your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only changed it once to Grooten. It was difficult with the children. I had to stay up nights and nights to teach them another name. Of course, they went to different schools too so that they couldn't talk to each other. In case children talked to each other, other children listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one went to one school, and the other went to the other school. So I had-- in the morning, I took one to one school, and then I took the other to the other school. And then I went to the fish market where you had to stand with a bucket for a few fish they gave you on a quota. Did your children have any sense of what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. They didn't even know they were Jewish the whole war.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were-- what kind of school did you put them into?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- they-- it [INAUDIBLE]. It was a Catholic school where they used to wear these caps with all sorts of badges on them. It was a Catholic school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the advantage of changing your name to Grooten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because our name was in the registry as De Groot. You know, when you live in-- like here, your name is registered here. Your name is-- they know exactly where you are. They know your address here, the government, everybody knows has to know where you live. And that was the same in Belgium. So they had my name. They had everything. They-- Jewish, we had the Jewish star. So we had to change our name.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin to wear the Jewish star in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- that was in 1942. We had to wear it on the dress, on your underwear, on everything you put on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who gave it to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what way was it distributed?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to go to the German office and get them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel to se-- did you sew it yourself on your clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you had to sew it yourself. And my oldest son was six. He had to wear one. But my youngest son was too young. They didn't have to wear it under the-- under six.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but my youngest son, he cried when he saw his brother has that on him. He cried so, that I had to sew one on his too. He thought it was fun. That was fun. [CHUCKLING] Did you-- how did you feel wearing that star?

speaker: interviewee
text: The star was terrible because I had friends who were also Catholic. And they were so scared that they would get picked up too, which they would have done. That nobody dared to look at you or help you in the street, or help you with anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been very disappointing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were boycotted from everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did those same people say things to you that-- was there anti-Semitism from them that you had never heard before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We never thought that would-- that anybody could not look at you in the streets. Friends of yours just passed you by. But you could see they were scared. The Germans made everybody scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are exceptions to those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, Dinneke, the girl who, who lived with-- my maid, she was a friend. She saved our lives lots of times, getting food. She went to get food for us and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about where you lived when you went to, to, to Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- you were there for--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite a big apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- about-- maybe about a year. And we got a lot of people coming from Holland, Jewish people who came from Holland. And they passed through our flats because we knew of places where they could go to also to live without--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you aware of those places?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- one tells the other, you know? One friend tells the other, here's a place they can go to, if you know anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about the Jewish-- about the resistance movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: The resistance?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were good. We had one friend, Victor, Victor Delme. He was the commissioner of Ixelles, where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- Ixelles was near--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a suburb of Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: A suburb of Brussels, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very nice place. I lived upstairs. There was a hairdresser downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. How did you meet Victor Delme?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she used to work in the [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a big store, like Nordstrom, something like that. She used to be a sales lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: A department store.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Louis used to go there. She used to sell him pens secretly so that he could make some money. So she became a very good friend of ours. She knew we were Jewish. So she used to come and help me a lot. And when Ronnie was caught, she brought food. She helped me an awful lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Louise was caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the police--

speaker: interviewee
text: Victor?

speaker: interviewer
text: Victor. Yes he used to-- he used to come, and he used to have false identity cards for our people. Not only Jewish people, but-- Now what was his-- one second-- what was his position in the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was just a policeman in the, in the police force.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you become familiar with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through Victor, was a friend of Victor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice guy. Very, very, very courageous. They went into the, into a camp in Brussels, in the German prison, and they got, they got out prisoners, the Belgian prisons. Policeman under false pretenses with German costumes on and everything. They had-- they were very courageous.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- were there Jews among them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were all--

speaker: interviewee
text: These were all Belgians, Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, did you participate, personally, in saving people's lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- yeah. We had passports given to us, false passports. And we had-- we made a lamp. And it was made of chintz. And there was a big seam--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it made of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chinz, you know chintz?

speaker: interviewer
text: Chinz? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, it was-- we didn't know you could see through it when the light was on in the evening. And we made a big seam like that. And we threaded the passports into the seam to hide them. Well, when-- when somebody came to visit us one night, we had to put the light on. You could see them all way around the light. Lucky enough, it was either somebody who didn't want to see them or didn't even notice it. So after, we took them away quick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with those passports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one, one [? pass ?] we gave to somebody who needed it. I don't know who it was anymore, but they were caught anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pay for those passports?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't pay for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were, they were meant for people who couldn't afford to pay for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to survive when your husband was taken away? What did you do for food, for money?

speaker: interviewee
text: For money, my friend, [? Fina ?] used to bring us some food. And Victor, the-- and [NON-ENGLISH], the other policeman, the other policeman, he used to bring us a little money now and again. And Dinneke, she got money from home and she used to go and buy food. I don't know how I existed, but I existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dinneke continued to live with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the whole war. And after the war, we gave her that little house we had from my mother in Antwerp. So she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in touch with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: So after the war, she had the little house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in touch with her now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's dead. She died a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody's dead. She had a daughter called Diane and a boy called Joseph. And she-- they wrote to me for a while. We used to write backwards and forwards. And then she got married. And-- and then I never heard from her again. I wrote to the address, but there wasn't any answer anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that at one point during the war, you lived on top of a police station.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was my mother and father-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when Louis was caught, I packed my bags at once, and I went over to my mother and father-in-law. I thought maybe if he's caught, they will torture him, or he'll say something that will lead them to us in that small flat. So I left the flat at once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the sol-- did the police know that you were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. We had false passports. And the ones that did know, they were, they were people who didn't like the idea-- didn't like the rules that were going on. So they didn't take-- and if they did know, they didn't take any notice. Maybe they didn't know, maybe they did know. We'll never know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, what was life like as a mother with small children in Brussels at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewer
text: For yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, myself?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get through the day while your husband was in-- taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I really don't know. We got-- I know we got through it-- [? Tina, ?] used to take the children to school, in case they would think there's something wrong. And she used to go and bring them back. And I don't think I went out of the house very much because I couldn't speak the language properly. And I had an English accent when I did speak, so I couldn't really-- sometimes I used to go and visit a friend. Once we went over to the friend with my children. And the train we went on was halted, and two German soldiers came out with chains around their necks. And we had-- they were standing like that. And women had to go this side with children, and men, this side. Of course, that was a newspaper that the underground used to give out. And there were people who used to pass it around to people. And they were looking for these people, the Germans. So everybody had to get out, and they searched you. And I know my youngest son had little toy pistol, a wooden one. So I joked about the pistol. I joked, and I had to show my identity cards. So he said you get on. Get on with the children. I don't know if he knew anything or not, but he passed me and let me get on the train again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jews taken off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea what was happen-- what had happened to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I knew because of the-- when he was first in, we used to go-- I used to pack him food in jam jars. And I used to pack him in little bullion blocks. You know, those little soup squares? We used to pry it open, and we used to put a little money in it, or a little, little stubs of cigarettes, which they couldn't get, and cover it up with jam. And-- and when he got-- Louis knew there was something hidden it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get those packages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes-- the first time, he didn't know there was anything hidden in it, so he gave it away to other people too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: He got it-- we used to go-- I used to go with Dinneke. She used to say she was his fiancee. She used to go in and bring, bring the things and left it for, for the De Groot. And I sat at the end of the street with the children waiting for her to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Brussels, in Ixelles,

speaker: interviewer
text: When he was taken to Mechelen, were you able to visit him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I couldn't visit. Otherwise, I would've been there too. No. They couldn't have visitors. They were in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an idea of how long he would be gone for?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I-- I never thought he would come back. But an aunt of-- Louis' aunt-- she always used to read tea cups. She was Catholic lady. I used to go visit her. She helped me a lot. And she used to read tea-- tea leaves. And she said, Mimi, you'll always fall on your feet, and your husband's coming back. And don't have to worry, he'll be back. And he did come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did he return to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was that? Didn't he say a date? I think it was-- it was something before my mother-in-law's birthday--

speaker: interviewee
text: 28Th of April, she's-- her birthday. So I think it was just around near there that he came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really-- I don't remember. I can't say with certainty which dates it were anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did we approach you? Where were you when he came home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at-- I think I was at my mother-in-laws. Where was I when he came home? I was at home, in my-- in the little flat, where I always was. He came back there. We told him I was there. I think that's the way it went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was just fat. He had a head swollen up. He was so fat all over. They'd-- he'd only had turnips to eat, and then they get swelled up from turnips. He looked awful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he look much older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I couldn't-- I didn't know him, even, he was so changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the children do when they saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they was-- they, they really didn't know what was happening. My eldest son may have known more than my youngest son. But I kept that as much as I could from them because we had to lead a normal life. And if they knew anything, they would be liable to talk to somebody, or let something loose. Of course, the Gestapo used to live around the corner. And one day, I went into the bedroom, my children made all little paper balls like this. And they were lighting them up with matches, leaning over the window, waiting for the Gestapo to go by so that they could-- so they could throw the fireballs at them. I just caught them in time.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CHUCKLING] That was funny, wasn't I? Horrible. Terrifying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrifying.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you tell them when they asked, where's my father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I just told them, he's away. He'll be back. He's away. They really didn't understand, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: They were still very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they didn't know-- they didn't, they didn't know they were Jewish in the first place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when Louis came home, how did life change for the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it changed a lot. My husband was scared to go in the street. And we had a false cupboard made at the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we heard all the footsteps-- I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that false cupboard.

speaker: interviewee
text: How?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had a big cupboard, crockery cupboard. And we made a false door with hooks on the outside. And we hanged the pots and pans on the hooks. And when we heard footsteps, or, or the doorbell went and we didn't know who it was, we had to stuff him into the cupboard, in the back. Then we put this board with hooks on it, and all the pots and pans, so it was a false back to it. And he was hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to hide him there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. The children knew that too. They didn't know-- they thought that was fun. They thought it was-- I don't know what children think. If you talk to my eldest son, he can tell you more about that-- his thoughts-- than I, than I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it often that you had to hide him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too often, four or five times. And then we had a roof. We lived on the top floor, and there was a flat roof. And so then my husband could get some color, wouldn't look so white from being inside always. We used to have a rope, and he used to climb up the rope onto the roof with my youngest son. And we used to make little pots of food. And we used to hand it up to him, the food, [INAUDIBLE] up to him. That's how he'd spend some days when it was nice weather to get color in his cheeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would imagine getting a suntan in the middle of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- yeah. You think of all these things that-- if somebody did come, and he was so white, and so white, they'd think, what's happened to him? You think of all sorts of silly, funny things. Those are survival techniques.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the days-- mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you continue to stay in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in Brussels until--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the end of the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the war was over. Tell us what was-- what was that like? How did you know the war had, had finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, boy. You know, the good word travels quick. We knew that the English and the Canadians were coming in, and we could see the Germans pulling out. There was a panic. The Germans were all pulling out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people do when they saw the soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they were pulling out, we went over to the, to the barracks where they lived, and they had planted potatoes and onions, the Germans themselves. And we didn't have anything. And when we heard they were going, we, at once, went over the fields. And lots of people started digging up the potatoes and the onions. And then all of a sudden, we heard shots. And my youngest son, he had a big onion. He was that little. He found an onion that big and he wouldn't let lose of it. He wouldn't run. And my eldest son ran the other way. We ran there. I had to go back, kick the onion out of his hand, and drag him along. He didn't-- and he started crying. He didn't want to lose his onion. And the Germans were shooting at us all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you made it back to your apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we escaped. We weren't shot, thank God. But a lot of people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war was finished, tell us about the celebrations.

speaker: interviewee
text: About the liberation?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that wasn't-- that must have been the happiest day of all our lives. Come through with two small children who talk, and you never know who they're going to talk to.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop. OK, good, Mimi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that--
